<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

payload.text:
**Explaining why `any` appears despite strict mode**

Strict mode allows `any` if types are not properly inferred; here, `actionPage` is `any` likely because route type generation isn't wired or `queryClient.ensureQueryData` returns `any`. Explicitly typing `Route.useLoaderData` with generics may fix this if supported, but the deeper fix is enabling route type generation and ensuring `@tanstack/react-router` types are included in tsconfig.

**Clarifying why type errors don’t appear with `any`**

TypeScript’s strict mode doesn’t flag explicit `any` like the one from `Route.useLoaderData()` because it’s not implicit; this usually results from missing route typegen inclusion or route registration. Fixing requires ensuring generated route types are included in tsconfig and properly loaded, then adding explicit loader return types or type assertions to avoid untyped `any` usages.

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

**Analyzing type issues around queryClient and route typings**

Noticed that typing problems stem from untyped or loosely typed `queryClient` in loader context and that the generated route file disables type checking and uses `any`. The key fix may be enabling proper type generation for routes to restore correct inference instead of relying on `@ts-nocheck`-ed generated code. Next, inspecting the routeTree.gen file will clarify this.

**Identifying missing return types as source of `any` propagation**

Found that lacking explicit return types on `useGetOneQueryOptions` causes `any` to propagate through query options and loader data, undermining type inference. The route loader’s declared return type conflicts with this, so adding explicit typings on these hooks should restore correct types. Next step is to verify `useGetOneQueryOptions` typing.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Analyzing type issues in route loader data**

The `any` type for `actionPage` likely comes from missing or unrecognized route type generation, causing `useLoaderData()` to default to `any`. Ensuring the generated route types file is properly included in the TS program and running the type generation steps should restore strong typing.

**Explaining why Route types are any**

`actionPage` is `any` because `Route` is `any`, which happens when route types from `routeTree.gen.ts` aren’t correctly included in the TypeScript program. The fix is ensuring `routeTree.gen.ts` is part of your TS project—by adding it to `tsconfig` includes, referencing it via a `.d.ts` file, and running the TanStack Router type generation. Since `any` is allowed by TS strict settings, linter rules are needed to flag it explicitly.

**Analyzing type issues in route loader data**

The `any` type for `actionPage` likely comes from missing or unrecognized route type generation, causing `useLoaderData()` to default to `any`. Ensuring the generated route types file is properly included in the TS program and running the type generation steps should restore strong typing.

**Explaining why Route types are any**

`actionPage` is `any` because `Route` is `any`, which happens when route types from `routeTree.gen.ts` aren’t correctly included in the TypeScript program. The fix is ensuring `routeTree.gen.ts` is part of your TS project—by adding it to `tsconfig` includes, referencing it via a `.d.ts` file, and running the TanStack Router type generation. Since `any` is allowed by TS strict settings, linter rules are needed to flag it explicitly.

payload.message:
Short version: `strict: true` does **not** prohibit `any`. It only errors on *implicit* `any` you declare yourself. If `any` comes from a library type, `as any`, or a generated file with `// @ts-nocheck`, TypeScript will happily let it flow.

In your case, `actionPage` is `any` because `Route.useLoaderData()` is `any`. That almost always means TanStack Router’s file‑route typegen isn’t being picked up by the TS program used for this file. When that happens, `createFileRoute(...)` falls back to `any`, so all downstream types become `any`.

**What to check/fix**

1. Make sure the generated types are in the TS program.  
   Your repo has `src/routeTree.gen.ts`. If your editor or `tsc` isn’t loading it, the augmentation won’t apply.

   Quick fix (if your editor is using a different tsconfig): add a reference file, e.g. `src/router-types.d.ts`:

   ```ts
   /// <reference path="./routeTree.gen.ts" />
   ```

   This forces the augmentation to be included.

2. Confirm the route tree is being generated by the router plugin.  
   `tanstackStart()` in `vite.config.ts` should do this. Run `pnpm dev` or regenerate the route tree if it’s stale.

3. If you want **errors on any**, TypeScript won’t do that alone.  
   Use Biome/ESLint rule (e.g. `noExplicitAny`) to forbid it.

If you want, I can check the TS program setup your editor is using and wire in the `routeTree.gen.ts` reference so `Route.useLoaderData()` becomes strongly typed again.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.message:
In this repo, `routeTree.gen.ts` is generated by the TanStack Start Vite plugin (`tanstackStart()` in `vite.config.ts`). It’s produced automatically on **dev** or **build**.

So you can generate it by running either:

```sh
pnpm dev
```

or

```sh
pnpm build
```

The output file should be `src/routeTree.gen.ts`.

If you want, I can run the command here and confirm the file updates.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Examining route typing issues**

The actionPage typings likely show `any` because the routeTree.gen.ts file with key module augmentations has `// @ts-nocheck` but remains included and should still apply augmentations; this suggests the disconnect is in how createFileRoute's generics depend on these augmentations, which seem present but possibly not fully picked by TS server.

**Investigating type resolution for file routes**

The route's `useLoaderData` returns `any` because the `Route` itself is typed as `any`, likely due to the `createFileRoute` call not properly linking to the `FileRoutesByPath` augmentation from `routeTree.gen.ts`. I'm verifying if module resolution and package hoisting affect the visibility of `@tanstack/react-router` types needed for correct inference.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
